proposer_reasoning: The query filters on production_year with a range condition (BETWEEN 2000 AND 2010) and is currently doing a sequential scan. A B-tree index on production_year will enable efficient index range scans, directly addressing the p99 latency bottleneck for this read-heavy workload.

With the production_year index already applied, the planner may still misestimate cardinality for range queries if production_year and kind_id are correlated (e.g., certain kinds of titles cluster in certain years). Extended statistics will help the planner produce better row estimates and choose optimal plans for queries filtering on production_year, especially when combined with kind_id joins or filters.

The existing single-column index on production_year should already support the range scan. A composite index covering (production_year, kind_id) can serve as a covering index for queries that filter by production_year and also need kind_id, potentially enabling index-only scans and reducing heap fetches for common join patterns with kind_type.

The cast_info table has ~300k rows and no secondary indexes. Given the read-heavy OLTP workload with joins to title, an index on movie_id (foreign key to title) will accelerate join lookups when queries filter titles by production_year and need to retrieve cast information.

The problem query on title.production_year already has an index applied. Given the schema and typical OLTP join patterns, cast_info is frequently joined to the name table via person_id. Adding this index will accelerate person-based lookups and joins that are common in read-heavy movie database workloads.

The title table already has indexes on production_year and related columns. For read-heavy OLTP with joins involving movie_keyword (common in keyword-based title lookups), adding an index on movie_keyword.movie_id will accelerate joins between title and movie_keyword, which is a common pattern in this schema. This is a targeted, reversible fix for the join-heavy workload.

The problem query joins cast_info on movie_id and groups by title.id. With analytical scans appearing and bad row-count estimates mentioned, the planner likely underestimates cardinality when joining cast_info. Extended statistics on (movie_id, role_id) will help the planner understand the correlation between which movies have cast entries and their role distributions, improving join cardinality estimates and reducing plan flips.

The problem query joins cast_info on movie_id and groups by title.id. With analytical scans appearing and bad row-count estimates mentioned, the planner likely misjudges the distribution of cast_info rows per movie. Extended statistics on (movie_id, role_id) will help the planner better estimate cardinality for joins and grouping operations involving cast_info, reducing plan flips in this shifting workload regime.

With a drifting workload (0.42) and analytical scans appearing, the planner is producing bad row-count estimates leading to plan flips. Increasing default_statistics_target from the default 100 to 400 will capture more histogram buckets and MCV values during ANALYZE, improving cardinality estimates across all tables—especially for range predicates like production_year BETWEEN 2000 AND 2010. This is a session/system-level change that improves estimate quality broadly without requiring per-column tuning.

With analytical scans emerging and the problematic query doing a GROUP BY with COUNT(*) aggregation over a join, the planner may be choosing suboptimal hash/sort strategies due to memory pressure. Increasing work_mem to 256MB allows hash aggregates and hash joins to complete in-memory rather than spilling to disk, which directly addresses the p99 latency spikes from plan instability during aggregation. Given the existing indexes on cast_info(movie_id) and title(production_year), the join access path is covered, but the aggregation phase benefits from more memory.

The problem query joins title.id to cast_info.movie_id while filtering on title.production_year. The planner is misestimating how many title.id values match the year range, leading to bad join cardinality estimates. Extended statistics on (id, production_year) will help the planner understand the functional dependency between the PK and the filter column, improving row count estimates for the join output.

The query joins title.id to cast_info.movie_id while filtering on title.production_year. The planner needs to estimate how many distinct title.id values exist within the 2000-2010 production_year range to properly size the join result. Extended statistics on (id, production_year) will give the planner functional-dependency and ndistinct information to produce accurate row-count estimates for this filtered join pattern, fixing the bad cardinality estimate that causes plan instability.

With hash joins spilling and plan flips despite work_mem at 256MB, the planner is likely overestimating sequential scan costs relative to index usage, causing erratic plan choices under concurrent load. Lowering random_page_cost from the default 4.0 to 1.5 better reflects modern SSD/cached workloads, stabilizing the planner toward index-based nested loops for the 5-table join and reducing the plan regressions driving p99 volatility.

With work_mem at 256MB and hash joins still spilling under the 5-table join workload, raising hash_mem_multiplier to 3.0 gives hash operations up to 768MB before spilling to disk. This directly addresses the "hash joins spilling" symptom without requiring a service bounce (it's a session-level GUC), and may stabilize plans by making hash joins consistently viable rather than causing plan flips when estimates vary.

With hash joins spilling despite work_mem at 256MB, the 5-table join needs more memory for hash operations specifically. hash_mem_multiplier (default 2.0) scales work_mem for hash-based operations. Setting to 3.0 gives hash joins up to 768MB, reducing spills and stabilizing plans under concurrent write pressure without requiring a service bounce.

With work_mem already at 256MB but hash joins still spilling under the 5-table join workload, increasing hash_mem_multiplier from default 2.0 to 4.0 gives hash operations up to 1GB (4×256MB) before spilling to disk. This directly targets the hash join spill issue causing p99 volatility without requiring a service bounce—it can be set per-session or via ALTER SYSTEM + reload.

With work_mem already at 256MB but hash joins still spilling under concurrent 5-table joins, the hash_mem_multiplier (default 2.0) limits hash operations to 512MB. Raising it to 4.0 allows hash tables up to 1GB, reducing spill-to-disk for these large multi-table joins. This is a session-level parameter that can be tuned without restart and directly addresses the observed hash join spilling causing plan instability.

With mixed OLTP+OLAP storm causing p99 oscillation 80ms->900ms, the bulk inserts are likely causing WAL flush contention that blocks the 5-table join queries. Setting synchronous_commit=off eliminates fsync wait on commit, trading a small durability window (~600ms of potential data loss on crash) for dramatically reduced write latency and contention. This is reversible and less destructive than fsync=off or VACUUM FULL, while directly addressing the write/read contention pattern.
human_rationale: This is a genuinely defensible call despite the high drift and pressure environment. The change — setting synchronous_commit=off — is fully reversible with a single GUC flip (no DDL, no data migration, no structural side effects), meaning blast radius is bounded and rollback is instantaneous. The risk profile is well-understood: in exchange for up to one WAL write cycle (~200ms by default) of potential data loss on a hard crash, PostgreSQL eliminates the synchronous fsync round-trip on every COMMIT, which is a known and frequently decisive fix for commit-latency-driven p99 spikes exactly like the one described. The workload regime break (drift=0.89) and unprecedented p99 x10 spike strongly suggest the bottleneck is I/O-bound commit latency under an unfamiliar write pattern — precisely the scenario where this setting pays off most. The durability trade-off is real but narrow: it does not risk data corruption, does not risk loss of already-acknowledged bulk data, and does not affect read consistency or replication topology. Workloads that are truly crash-critical (financial ledgers, payment commits) should be evaluated, but for an active p99 incident with no other levers working, the reversibility, speed of effect, and well-characterized risk make this the right call. Approve with the instruction to: (1) set at the session or database level first if feasible to scope exposure, (2) monitor p99 and crash-recovery posture for 15 minutes, and (3) be prepared to revert immediately if the workload is confirmed to require strict durability guarantees.

proposer_reasoning: With p99 oscillating 80ms→900ms under mixed OLTP+OLAP with bulk inserts, full_page_writes is likely the bottleneck. After each checkpoint, Postgres writes entire 8KB pages on first modification, dramatically increasing I/O during insert storms. Disabling this eliminates that overhead. Risk is partial-page writes on crash, but with synchronous_commit=off already proposed, the durability posture is already relaxed. This is the highest-impact systemic lever remaining that directly addresses bulk insert I/O pressure.
human_rationale: I am rejecting this proposal despite the severe performance pressure. Disabling `full_page_writes` eliminates the post-checkpoint page-image writes that protect against partial-page corruption during a crash — if the instance dies between a checkpoint and the next WAL flush while this is off, we can end up with torn pages and an unrecoverable data file, requiring restore from backup. While the parameter IS technically reversible (a `SET` or `postgresql.conf` change + reload), the *damage* it enables is irreversible: a single poorly-timed OS/hardware failure during the window it is off can corrupt the cluster permanently. The current regime_break context (drift=0.95, unprecedented workload, p99 ~10x spike) actually makes this MORE dangerous, not less — unusual workload patterns increase I/O pressure and the probability of a crash or storage hiccup occurring during the exposure window. The performance benefit (reducing WAL volume by eliminating full-page images) is real but secondary to data integrity; the write amplification from full_page_writes is not the bottleneck class that causes 10x p99 latency spikes — those are overwhelmingly caused by lock contention, missing indexes, autovacuum bloat, connection saturation, or working-set/buffer-hit-ratio collapse. We should instead pursue: (1) `pg_stat_activity` / `pg_locks` triage to identify the dominant wait event, (2) `shared_buffers` or `effective_cache_size` tuning, (3) autovacuum aggressiveness / manual VACUUM on bloated tables, (4) connection pooling (PgBouncer) if connection count is spiking, or (5) read replica offload. Turning off `full_page_writes` under a high-drift, high-stress, unprecedented-workload regime is a data-integrity gamble with catastrophic downside that far outweighs the speculative write-latency gain.

params.detail: VACUUM FULL cast_info to eliminate bloat and reclaim fragmented pages from interleaved bulk inserts

proposer_reasoning: With mixed OLTP+OLAP storm causing bulk inserts interleaved with the 5-table join, cast_info (~300k rows, largest table) likely has severe bloat causing sequential and index scans to read far more pages than necessary. VACUUM FULL rewrites the table compactly, which can dramatically reduce I/O for both the join queries and index maintenance during inserts. This is the most impactful remaining lever given all targeted indexes and GUC tuning are exhausted.
human_rationale: I am rejecting this proposal despite the acute performance pressure. Here is my reasoning: VACUUM FULL on `cast_info` acquires an exclusive ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock for its entire duration, which will completely block all reads and writes to that table — a user-facing, high-traffic table in what is already a crisis regime. At drift=1.00 (unprecedented workload with p99 spiking ~10x), we have zero reliable historical baseline for how long this operation will take, and the lock hold time could easily stretch from minutes to hours depending on table size and I/O saturation. This makes the blast radius systemic: the cure could be worse than the disease, turning a latency spike into a full outage. The operation is also irreversible — once started, we cannot roll it back, and aborting partway leaves the table in an intermediate state. The stated justification (bloat from interleaved bulk inserts) is plausible but unvalidated; targeted indexes "not helping" does not confirm bloat is the root cause. Before committing to this, the team should: (1) quantify actual bloat via `pg_freespace` or `pgstattuple` to confirm this is the real bottleneck, (2) consider a standard (non-FULL) VACUUM or pg_repack/pg_squeeze which can run online without an exclusive lock, (3) schedule the operation during a confirmed low-traffic window with a tested rollback plan for dependent services. The pressure to act decisively is understandable, but an exclusive-lock full rewrite of a hot table during an active, unprecedented spike is exactly the scenario where a rushed irreversible action can cascade a bad situation into a catastrophe.

proposer_reasoning: The query filters on production_year with a range condition (BETWEEN 2000 AND 2010) and is doing a sequential scan. A B-tree index on production_year will enable efficient index range scans, drastically reducing I/O for this read-heavy workload.

With the production_year index already in place, the planner may still misestimate cardinality if production_year and kind_id are correlated (e.g., certain kinds of titles cluster in certain year ranges). Extended statistics on these two columns will improve row estimates and help the planner choose the index scan over a seq scan more reliably.

With ~300k rows in cast_info and no secondary indexes, queries joining title to cast_info on movie_id will require sequential scans. Adding an index on cast_info.movie_id enables efficient index lookups for joins from title, which is critical for the read-heavy OLTP workload pattern described.

With the production_year index already applied for the title table query, the next logical target is cast_info which has 300k rows and only a movie_id index. Adding a person_id index will support JOINs from name table lookups which are common in OLTP actor/cast queries, reducing seq scans on this large table.

The movie_keyword table lacks a secondary index on movie_id, which is a foreign key to title. In read-heavy OLTP workloads involving joins between title and movie_keyword (e.g., finding keywords for movies in a year range), this index will eliminate seq scans on movie_keyword and enable efficient index lookups for the join.

The movie_keyword table already has an index on movie_id but lacks one on keyword_id. For read-heavy OLTP workloads involving keyword lookups or joins from keyword to movie_keyword, this secondary index will eliminate seq scans and improve p99 latency on queries filtering/joining by keyword.

The production_year index already exists for the problem query. Adding an index on kind_id supports joins from title to kind_type and queries filtering by kind, which is a common pattern in this schema. This is a targeted, reversible fix for the read-heavy OLTP workload.

With the production_year index already applied, the seq scan on the title table should be resolved. The next optimization opportunity is improving cardinality estimates for cast_info joins. Extended statistics on movie_id and person_id will help the planner make better decisions when joining cast_info with title and name tables, which are common in read-heavy OLTP workloads involving actor/movie queries.

With the production_year index already in place on title, the seq scan issue should be resolved. However, the read-heavy OLTP workload likely includes joins between title and cast_info. Extended statistics on cast_info(movie_id, role_id) will help the planner make better cardinality estimates for queries filtering by both movie and role type, which is a common access pattern in this schema.

The index on production_year already exists, yet the query still shows a seq scan. This suggests the planner is misestimating row counts for the range filter. Extended statistics on production_year and title can help the planner better estimate the selectivity when both columns are involved in the query output, potentially improving cardinality estimates and encouraging index usage.

The problem query on title.production_year already has an index applied. The remaining untargeted table for extended statistics is movie_keyword, where correlation between movie_id and keyword_id can help the planner produce better cardinality estimates for joins involving keyword lookups, improving plan quality for read-heavy OLTP workloads.

The problem query joins title to cast_info on movie_id and groups by t.id. Bad row-count estimates suggest the planner misjudges the cast_info join cardinality. Extended statistics on (movie_id, nr_order) can help the planner understand the distribution of cast entries per movie (nr_order often correlates with movie_id as it indicates cast ordering within a movie), improving join cardinality estimates and reducing plan flips during this drifting workload regime.

The problem query joins title to cast_info on movie_id and groups by t.id. Bad rowcount estimates in this shifting workload with analytical scans suggest the planner is misestimating the join cardinality. Extended statistics on cast_info(movie_id, nr_order) can help the planner better understand the distribution of cast entries per movie, as nr_order (cast ordering) is correlated with movie_id. This should improve row count estimates for the GROUP BY aggregation and avoid plan flips between hash and nested loop joins.

The problem query joins title to cast_info filtering on production_year and grouping by title.id. The bad rowcount estimate likely stems from the planner misestimating how many cast_info rows match per movie. Extended statistics on (movie_id, nr_order) can help the planner understand the distribution of cast entries per movie (nr_order typically correlates with movie_id as it represents ordering within a movie's cast), improving join cardinality estimates and preventing plan flips.

With a shifting workload (drift=0.54) and bad row-count estimates on the title-cast_info join, the planner needs richer histogram data to improve cardinality estimates. Increasing default_statistics_target from 100 to 400 will gather more detailed statistics during ANALYZE, helping the planner make better decisions for range predicates like production_year BETWEEN 2000 AND 2010 and join selectivity estimates. This is a systemic but reversible change that addresses the root cause of plan flips in a drifting analytical workload.

Hash joins are spilling to disk causing latency variance and plan instability. Increasing work_mem allows hash tables to stay in memory, eliminating spill-induced latency spikes. This directly addresses the "hash joins spilling" symptom in the 5-table join workload without requiring a restart.

With work_mem already at 256MB and hash joins still spilling, the 5-table join under concurrent writes needs more memory specifically for hash operations. hash_mem_multiplier=4.0 allows hash joins up to 1GB (4x work_mem) which should eliminate spills and stabilize plan choices without increasing general sort/aggregate memory. This is the targeted lever for hash-join-heavy workloads experiencing spills and plan flips.
human_rationale: This is a GUC (runtime configuration) change to `hash_mem_multiplier`, raising it from the default (2.0) to 4.0. Critically, this change IS reversible — it can be reset session-level or cluster-wide via `SET`/`ALTER SYSTEM SET` with an immediate `pg_reload_conf()`, with no persistent structural side effects. The operating regime is turbulent (drift=0.71) with documented plan regressions on multi-table joins, memory pressure, and write-heavy bursts where single-column indexes have stopped moving p99 latency — a classic symptom of hash join/hash aggregate memory spilling to disk under the default multiplier. Raising `hash_mem_multiplier` to 4.0 doubles the memory budget for hash operations beyond `work_mem`, which directly targets this failure mode by allowing more in-memory hash joins and aggregations, reducing spill, and potentially resolving the planner regressions. The blast radius is legitimately high — if the system is already memory-pressured, a higher multiplier could exacerbate pressure across concurrent sessions simultaneously — but this risk is bounded and manageable: (1) it does not touch user-facing data or schema, (2) it is fully reversible in seconds with no downtime, (3) the alternative (continued p99 degradation under plan regressions) is an active ongoing incident. The recommended mitigation is to monitor `pg_stat_activity`, OS-level memory metrics, and query plan shapes immediately after applying, with a rollback threshold pre-agreed (e.g., if system memory utilization exceeds 85% or new OOM signals appear). Given reversibility, the active turbulent incident context, and the strong mechanistic fit between this parameter and the observed symptoms, approval is the defensible call.

proposer_reasoning: With plan regressions on a 5-table join and stale statistics under turbulent drift, the planner may be making suboptimal join-order decisions using genetic query optimizer (GEQO) or limited search. Raising join_collapse_limit from default 8 to 12 ensures the planner exhaustively searches join orderings for this 5-table query, stabilizing plans without memory-pressure risk. This is a session-safe GUC that can be tuned per-connection or globally with immediate effect and no restart.

With a mixed OLTP+OLAP storm where bulk inserts are interleaved with complex 5-table joins, the p99 oscillation (80ms->900ms) strongly suggests write-path contention causing WAL flush stalls. Setting synchronous_commit=off eliminates per-commit fsync waits, dramatically reducing write latency variance. This is technically reversible and trades a small durability window (~600ms of potential data loss on crash) for immediate throughput stabilization. Given all targeted indexes and statistics are exhausted, this is the safest systemic lever before irreversible options like VACUUM FULL.
human_rationale: I'm approving this change, but with clear eyes about the trade-offs. The situation: drift=0.90 (unprecedented workload), p99 latency ~10x elevated, targeted index work has already been tried and failed to resolve the spike. The proposed change — setting synchronous_commit=off — is a well-understood PostgreSQL GUC that trades the small window of potential data loss (last ~1–3 WAL flush cycles, typically <200ms of committed transactions) for a significant reduction in commit latency by eliminating the synchronous WAL fsync round-trip. Critically, this is REVERSIBLE — it can be set back to 'on' at session or cluster level instantly with a single SET command or postgresql.conf reload, with no restart required. The blast radius is "high" in the sense that it affects all writes cluster-wide, but the actual durability risk is bounded and well-documented (not corruption, just potential loss of last few commits on a hard crash). Given: (1) the workload is in an unprecedented regime actively causing a p99 crisis, (2) indexes have not helped, suggesting the bottleneck is commit/WAL throughput rather than query execution, (3) the change is immediately reversible with no structural side effects, (4) the bounded durability risk is an accepted PostgreSQL operational pattern for high-throughput scenarios, and (5) the cost of inaction (continued 10x p99 spike) is itself high-blast-radius to users — approval is the defensible call. Post-approval actions required: (a) immediately document the change and notify the team, (b) set a hard timer (≤2 hours) to reassess and revert once the regime stabilizes or a more durable fix is identified, (c) ensure application teams are aware that a crash in this window could lose a small amount of recent commits, and (d) monitor replication lag if standbys are in use, as remote_apply/remote_write semantics will also be relaxed.

params.detail: VACUUM FULL cast_info; -- reclaims bloat and rewrites table for sequential scan efficiency during mixed OLTP+OLAP storm

proposer_reasoning: With ~300k rows, mixed bulk inserts and 5-table joins are likely causing severe table bloat in cast_info (the largest table). VACUUM FULL rewrites the table compactly, restoring sequential scan performance and reducing I/O amplification. The operation takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock but on a 300k-row table should complete in seconds. This addresses the root cause of p99 oscillation: fragmented heap pages forcing excessive random I/O during the join's inner loops.
human_rationale: VACUUM FULL on `cast_info` is an exclusive-lock, full-table-rewrite operation that will block ALL reads and writes on that table for its entire duration — which, for a large cast_info table in an IMDB-scale schema, could run for minutes to hours. During an active p99 spike crisis with a regime_break at maximum drift (1.00), this is precisely the wrong moment to take a table offline: it would convert a latency crisis into a full availability outage for any query touching cast_info. The reversibility flag is correctly set to False — once the lock is acquired and the rewrite begins, there is no safe abort path without leaving the table in an inconsistent intermediate state. The rationale that bloat is causing sequential scan inefficiency is plausible but unproven in this unprecedented workload regime, and VACUUM FULL does not address index bloat, plan cache issues, connection saturation, or the many other more likely causes of a 10x p99 spike. Safer, non-blocking alternatives must be exhausted first: (1) pg_repack or VACUUM (without FULL) to reclaim dead tuples without an exclusive lock; (2) query cancellation or work_mem/parallel-query tuning to relieve the OLAP pressure; (3) connection pooling adjustments or read replica offload; (4) identifying and killing the specific long-running queries driving the spike. The combination of maximum regime uncertainty, irreversibility, exclusive table lock, and high blast radius (user-facing availability loss) makes this an unacceptable risk. Reject.

proposer_reasoning: The query filters on production_year and groups by id. The planner is getting bad row-count estimates because it doesn't understand the correlation between id distribution and production_year ranges. Extended statistics on (id, production_year) will help the planner estimate how many title rows fall in the 2000-2010 range and improve join cardinality estimates, reducing plan flips in this shifting-workload regime.

Hash joins spilling to disk and plan instability on 5-table joins under write-heavy load points directly to insufficient work_mem. Increasing from default (4MB) to 256MB allows hash tables to stay in memory, eliminating disk spills and stabilizing plan choices. This is a session-level GUC that can be tuned without restart and rolled back instantly.

With mixed OLTP+OLAP and bulk inserts causing p99 oscillation from 80ms to 900ms, the bottleneck is likely WAL flush latency during commit storms. Setting synchronous_commit=off eliminates fsync wait at commit time, trading a small durability window (~600ms of transactions could be lost on crash) for dramatically lower and more stable commit latency. This is reversible and less dangerous than fsync=off, while directly addressing the insert-interleaved workload pattern.

params.detail: VACUUM FULL cast_info; -- reclaims bloat, rewrites table contiguously for better sequential scan and join performance under mixed OLTP+OLAP storm

proposer_reasoning: With ~300k rows and no secondary indexes, cast_info is the largest table driving the 5-table join. Mixed bulk inserts have likely caused significant table bloat and fragmentation. VACUUM FULL rewrites the table contiguously, dramatically improving sequential scan performance and reducing I/O variance that causes the 80ms→900ms oscillation. This is irreversible but addresses the root cause when targeted indexes haven't helped.
human_rationale: Rejecting this proposal despite the acute performance crisis. VACUUM FULL on `cast_info` is an exclusive-lock operation — it acquires AccessExclusiveLock for the entire duration of the rewrite, blocking ALL reads and writes on that table until completion. On a large table like `cast_info` (a core IMDb-schema table with potentially hundreds of millions of rows), this could mean minutes to tens of minutes of complete table unavailability, which in an already-degraded p99-spiking environment would constitute a self-inflicted outage on top of the existing incident. The change is also irreversible in the sense that once the lock is held and the rewrite begins, you cannot safely abort without leaving the system in a worse state. The regime_break context (drift=0.88) is the single most important signal here: we are in unprecedented workload territory, which means our historical estimates of table size, lock contention duration, and recovery behavior are all unreliable — the blast radius is genuinely unknown and likely underestimated. The stated rationale (reclaiming bloat for sequential scan / join performance) is a plausible long-term optimization, but bloat is almost certainly not the root cause of a sudden 10x p99 spike in a novel workload regime. Better immediate alternatives exist that do not require an exclusive lock: (1) `pg_repack` or `VACUUM` (non-FULL) to reclaim dead tuples without blocking; (2) workload routing/throttling to reduce OLAP query concurrency; (3) query-level timeouts or connection pooling adjustments to shed load; (4) targeted partial indexes or covering indexes on the specific join/scan patterns driving the spike. A VACUUM FULL should be planned for a low-traffic maintenance window with proper runbook, not executed under fire in an unprecedented drift scenario.

proposer_reasoning: With hash joins spilling and plan flips on multi-table joins, the planner is likely overestimating random I/O cost and flip-flopping between hash and nested loop plans. Lowering random_page_cost from default 4.0 to 1.5 encourages more consistent index usage and hash join behavior, reducing plan regressions. This is a session-safe GUC that can be tuned without restart and rolled back instantly.

The query filters on production_year and groups by id. With analytical scans appearing and bad row-count estimates explicitly noted, the planner likely misestimates the correlation between these columns. Extended statistics on (id, production_year) will give the planner better cardinality estimates for the filtered join, reducing plan flips and improving p99 latency without any blast radius concerns.

The problem query filters on production_year with a range condition (BETWEEN 2000 AND 2010) and is currently doing a seq scan. A B-tree index on production_year will allow an index scan or bitmap index scan, dramatically reducing I/O for this selective range query in a read-heavy OLTP workload.

The index on production_year is already applied but the query may still suffer from poor cardinality estimates when combining production_year ranges with kind_id joins. Extended statistics on these correlated columns will help the planner produce better estimates and choose optimal plans for range queries that also filter/join on kind_id.

The problem query on title.production_year already has an index applied. The cast_info table (300k rows, no secondary indexes) is likely involved in joins via movie_id -> title. Adding an index on cast_info(movie_id) will speed up joins from title to cast_info, which is a common pattern in this schema and a clear gap given no secondary indexes exist on cast_info yet.

With cast_info having ~300k rows and no secondary indexes except movie_id, queries joining or filtering on person_id (common in actor/name lookups) will seq scan. Adding an index on person_id enables efficient lookups for person-centric queries, a typical OLTP pattern for this schema.

The problem query joins cast_info to title filtering on production_year, causing bad row-count estimates. Extended statistics on cast_info(movie_id, role_id) helps the planner understand the correlation between which movies have which role types, improving cardinality estimates for the join and aggregation. This complements the existing movie_id index and addresses the analytical scan pattern emerging in the workload.

With work_mem at 256MB and hash joins still spilling, increasing hash_mem_multiplier from default 2.0 to 4.0 gives hash operations up to 1GB (4×256MB) before spilling to disk. This directly addresses the 5-table join spilling problem without affecting sort operations or other memory consumers as aggressively, and is session-tunable so it's safely reversible.

The 5-table join involves movie_keyword joining to title, but movie_keyword lacks an index on movie_id. Given cast_info indexes are already applied, this is the remaining join path bottleneck. A covering index here enables efficient nested loop joins from title and avoids sequential scans on movie_keyword during the OLTP+OLAP mix, which is likely contributing to the p99 oscillation.

The 5-table join involves movie_keyword linking title to keyword. With no secondary indexes on movie_keyword, each join probe requires a full scan. A composite index on (movie_id, keyword_id) enables index-only scans for the join and eliminates heap fetches, which is critical when bulk inserts are causing table bloat and competing for I/O. This is a targeted, reversible fix that hasn't been tried yet.

With plan flips on multi-table joins and memory pressure indicated, the planner may be underestimating available cache and choosing hash joins that spill instead of index-nested-loop plans. Raising effective_cache_size (a session-safe GUC, no restart needed) guides the planner toward index-based plans when indexes exist, reducing plan volatility and spill-related latency on the 5-table join workload.

The problem query joins cast_info on movie_id with a filtered title table. With analytical scans appearing and bad row-count estimates noted, the planner likely misjudges cardinality when estimating how many cast_info rows match the joined movies. Extended statistics on cast_info(movie_id, role_id) helps the planner understand the correlation between which movies have cast entries and their role distribution, improving join cardinality estimates and reducing plan flips.

The problem query on title.production_year already has an index applied. Looking at the schema, movie_keyword has a composite index on (movie_id, keyword_id) but lacks a standalone index on keyword_id. Queries filtering or joining on keyword_id alone cannot efficiently use the composite index. Adding this targeted index supports keyword-based lookups common in read-heavy OLTP workloads involving movie searches.

The production_year index already exists for the problem query. Since title.kind_id is a foreign key often used in joins with kind_type and may appear in WHERE clauses filtering by kind, adding this index will support efficient lookups and joins on the kind_id column, improving query performance for workloads involving title-kind relationships.

The problem query on title.production_year already has an index applied. Given the schema and read-heavy OLTP pattern, cast_info is heavily joined and filtered. An index on role_id enables efficient lookups when filtering by role type in actor/crew queries, which is a common pattern joining cast_info to other tables.

The problem query filters on production_year (already indexed) but does SELECT *. A covering index including 'id' can speed up index-only scans for queries that join on id after filtering by year, reducing heap fetches. This is a targeted, reversible enhancement to the existing production_year index strategy.
